questions.prompt: Why use adjusted prices for backtests with multi-year lookback?

choices.text: Splits and dividends create discontinuities in raw price series. A 2-for-1 split halves the printed price overnight — un-adjusted data shows AAPL going from $200 to $100, which looks like a catastrophic crash. Strategies that react to 'big moves' fire false signals; momentum, breakout, mean-reversion all degrade. Adjusted prices back-propagate splits so historical levels are comparable to today (the pre-split $200 becomes $100 retroactively). Adjusted-for-dividends prices reflect total return, which matters for long-hold backtests. Default to adjusted (yfinance: `auto_adjust=True`; Alpaca returns adjusted). Always confirm the data provider's adjustment policy before backtesting.

questions.explanation: Un-adjusted prices show false crashes at splits. Adjusted prices preserve continuity.

questions.prompt: Bot strategy holds positions for 2-3 days based on daily bars. What data should it pay for?

choices.text: Daily bars are sufficient — likely free via Alpaca, Yahoo, or IEX Cloud. Tick data is overkill for a swing strategy: holding period is days, decisions are on daily-close signals, paying for tick storage and bandwidth gives zero alpha while costing $99+/month. Match data granularity to the strategy's actual time horizon. Microstructure-sensitive strategies need ticks; daily/swing strategies need bars; multi-year quant research needs adjusted historical bars + occasional intraday for entry/exit timing. Pay only for what your strategy reads. Tick data is the most-expensive, most-overprescribed data tier in retail algo trading.

questions.explanation: Daily bars match the strategy timescale. Tick data is overkill and expensive for swing strategies.
